worldGlobe.defaultHoverNote: Drag to orbit. Scroll or pinch to zoom. Country dots are now painted from the flag colors of each individual nation.
worldGlobe.hoveredCountryNote: Every country is now color-woven from its own flag palette. Hover still locks the country and sharpens its atlas glow.
worldGlobe.loadError: Unable to initialize the world flag globe.

roadmap.hint: This visualization is already on the roadmap, but the interactive build is still locked.
roadmap.title: Implementation planned

graphFocus.bfsRouteHint: Whole graph shows the BFS discovery tree. This line shows one selected source-to-node route.
graphFocus.dfsBranchHint: Whole graph shows the DFS tree. This line shows one selected explored branch.
graphFocus.shortestPathHint: Whole graph shows the shortest-path tree. This line shows one selected endpoint route.

logPanel.emptyLabel: Run the scenario to start a chronological trace.

page.emptyLabel: No algorithms match the current filters.
page.heroDescription: Scan topics faster, compare complexity at a glance and jump into polished visual walkthroughs without losing context.
page.resultsSubtitle: Refine the current topic by difficulty and semantic traits without leaving the catalog context.
page.title: Algorithms

traits.emptyLabel: No semantic facets are available in the current topic.
traits.popoverSubtitle: Pick the properties and paradigms that matter for the current topic.

decisions.appendFinisher: append interval finisher to burst order
decisions.expandSubintervals: expand left and right subintervals first

descriptions.appendBurst: After both sides are solved, balloon #{{pivot}} bursts last for interval #{{left}}..#{{right}}.
descriptions.commitScore: Store best coin haul for interval #{{left}}..#{{right}} with balloon #{{pivot}} as the last burst.
descriptions.complete: Recovered one optimal burst order with {{coins}} coins.
descriptions.initialize: Pad the array with sentinel 1s so every interval can treat its chosen last balloon uniformly.
descriptions.inspectCandidate: Try keeping balloon #{{pivot}} as the last one burst inside interval #{{left}}..#{{right}}.
descriptions.traceSplit: Optimal interval #{{left}}..#{{right}} leaves balloon #{{pivot}} for the final pop in this subproblem.

descriptions.commitCount: Store the total number of ways to stand on stair {{step}}.
descriptions.complete: Finished counting all valid 1-step and 2-step climbs up to stair {{step}}.
descriptions.initialize: Seed the ground step and the first stair with one way each.
descriptions.inspectParents: Ways to reach stair {{step}} come from stair {{prev}} and stair {{prevPrev}}.

descriptions.backtrackSkip: Skip denomination {{coin}} and move to the previous row.
descriptions.backtrackTake: Take coin {{coin}} and stay on the same row because coins are unbounded.
descriptions.compareSkipTake: At amount {{amount}}, compare skipping coin {{coin}} with taking it again from the same row.
descriptions.forcedSkip: Coin {{coin}} cannot help amount {{amount}}, so only the skip branch survives.
descriptions.initialize: Initialize amount 0 with 0 coins and mark every other amount as unreachable before processing denominations.
descriptions.storeBest: Store minimum coins for amount {{amount}} using first {{row}} denomination(s).
descriptions.unreachable: Target {{target}} is unreachable with the available denominations.

labels.unboundedRowHint: unbounded take stays on same row

descriptions.backtrackCarry: {{char}} already matches, so move diagonally without cost.
descriptions.backtrackInsert: Inserting {{char}} gives the optimal predecessor from the left.
descriptions.backtrackReplace: Best path replaces {{leftChar}} with {{rightChar}}, then moves diagonally.
descriptions.compareOperations: Compare replace, delete and insert costs for {{leftChar}} -> {{rightChar}}.
descriptions.initialize: Seed the top row and left column with insert/delete counts.

descriptions.commitTerm: Store F({{index}}) in the table so later terms can reuse it instantly.
descriptions.complete: Tabulation finishes with F({{index}}) ready at the end of the strip.
descriptions.initialize: Seed F(0) = 0 and F(1) = 1 before tabulating larger terms.
descriptions.inspectParents: F({{index}}) is the sum of the two previous Fibonacci terms.

descriptions.backtrackChosen: {{item}} belongs to the optimal backpack, so jump diagonally by its weight {{weight}}.
descriptions.backtrackSkip: {{item}} is not part of the optimal backpack at this capacity, so move upward.
descriptions.compareSkipTake: At capacity {{cap}}, compare skipping {{item}} with taking it.
descriptions.complete: Optimal backpack value is {{value}} with {{count}} selected item(s).
descriptions.forcedSkip: {{item}} is too heavy for capacity {{cap}}, so only the skip branch is legal.
descriptions.initialize: Initialize a {{rows}} × {{cols}} table. Row 0 and capacity 0 form the base cases.

descriptions.backtrackLeft: Left neighbor is larger, so move left.
descriptions.backtrackMatch: {{sourceChar}} matches {{targetChar}}, so include it and move diagonally.
descriptions.backtrackUpward: Top neighbor is at least as strong, so move upward.
descriptions.complete: Backtracking finishes with longest common subsequence "{{sequence}}".
descriptions.initialize: Initialize the LCS table with zeroes on the top row and left column.

descriptions.blocked: Value {{from}} cannot extend into {{to}} because the sequence would stop increasing.
descriptions.canExtend: Value {{from}} can extend into {{to}}, so compare LIS length {{length}} + 1.
descriptions.chooseEndpoint: Pick index {{index}} as the LIS endpoint because it has the strongest length.
descriptions.commitPredecessor: Update LIS ending at index {{index}} to length {{length}} via predecessor index {{predecessor}}.
descriptions.complete: Recovered one longest increasing subsequence with length {{length}}.
descriptions.initialize: Initialize every position with subsequence length 1, because each value is a valid LIS by itself.
descriptions.lockResult: Finalize the best LIS that ends exactly at index {{index}}.

decisions.expandChildren: expand left and right child intervals

descriptions.initialize: Start with zero cost on the diagonal: multiplying one matrix alone costs nothing.
descriptions.inspectCandidate: Try split k = {{split}} for interval A{{left}}..A{{right}}.
descriptions.traceLeaf: A{{index}} is a leaf matrix in the optimal multiplication tree.
descriptions.traceSplit: Optimal interval A{{left}}..A{{right}} splits at k = {{split}}.

decisions.consumeTextAndRegex: consume one text char and one regex token
decisions.jumpLeftTwo: jump left by two pattern columns
decisions.moveUpKeepStar: move upward and keep star column

descriptions.commitStarState: Commit whether regex star group keeps the prefix match alive.
descriptions.commitToken: Commit whether token {{token}} covers text character {{char}}.
descriptions.complete: Recovered one valid regex derivation for "{{source}}" against "{{pattern}}".
descriptions.initialize: Seed dp[0][0] = true and prepare the top row for star groups that can match an empty prefix.
descriptions.inspectDot: Dot wildcard matches any single character, so only the diagonal predecessor matters.
descriptions.inspectLiteral: Literal token {{token}} only works if it matches {{char}}.
descriptions.inspectStarGroup: Star after {{token}} can either vanish or absorb another {{char}}.
descriptions.noMatch: The full text does not satisfy the regex pattern.
descriptions.propagateStarGroup: Regex star group {{token}}* may skip itself and keep the empty prefix matched.
descriptions.traceDirect: Token {{token}} matches {{char}}, so trace diagonally to the previous prefix state.
descriptions.traceStarConsume: Regex group {{token}}* consumes {{char}} and stays active for more characters.
descriptions.traceStarEmpty: Regex group {{token}}* collapses to zero occurrences here.

decisions.takeOpensNewReachableSum: take branch opens new reachable sum

descriptions.backtrackSkip: Sum {{sum}} was already reachable without {{value}}, so move upward and skip it.
descriptions.backtrackTake: {{value}} participates in the winning subset, so jump to sum {{sum}}.
descriptions.compareBranches: Check whether sum {{sum}} is reachable by skipping {{value}} or taking it.
descriptions.forcedSkip: {{value}} is larger than sum {{sum}}, so only the skip branch is legal.
descriptions.impossible: Target sum {{target}} is impossible with the available numbers.
descriptions.initialize: Seed sum 0 as reachable and mark every other sum unreachable before considering any numbers.
descriptions.storeReachability: Store reachability of sum {{sum}} after processing first {{row}} number(s).

decisions.consumeTextAndPattern: consume one text char and one pattern token
decisions.moveUpKeepStar: move upward and keep star active
decisions.starMustConsume: star must consume one more character

descriptions.commitStar: Commit whether star keeps the prefix match alive at dp[{{row}}][{{col}}].
descriptions.commitToken: Commit whether {{token}} can cover text position {{row}}.
descriptions.complete: Recovered one valid wildcard route for "{{source}}" against "{{pattern}}".
descriptions.initialize: Seed dp[0][0] = true and prepare the first row for leading wildcard stars.
descriptions.inspectLiteral: Literal pattern token {{token}} only works here if it equals {{char}}.
descriptions.inspectQuestion: Question mark matches any single character, so it checks the diagonal predecessor.
descriptions.inspectStar: Star at pattern column {{col}} can either absorb {{char}} or stay empty.
descriptions.noMatch: Full text and pattern do not match under wildcard rules.
descriptions.propagateLeadingStar: Leading "*" keeps the empty text prefix matchable through pattern column {{col}}.
descriptions.traceDiagonal: Token {{token}} matches {{char}}, so move diagonally to the predecessor state.
descriptions.traceStarConsume: Star absorbs character {{char}} and stays in the same pattern column.
descriptions.traceStarEmpty: Star also works as an empty span here, so move left in the pattern.

labels.leadingStarEmpty: leading star can match empty prefix

expressions.bwtOutput: L = last column of sorted rotations

notes.bwtOutput: This rearrangement is reversible, but far friendlier to downstream run-length style compression.
notes.insertionPoint: One more rotation snaps into its final sorted slot.
notes.rotationMatrix: The BWT sorts cyclic rotations, not suffixes.
notes.rowCompare: We use insertion-sort-like steps here purely to make the matrix movement readable.
notes.runComparison: Fewer, longer runs are exactly why BWT is so effective before RLE or entropy coding.

decisions.differentStartingPoints: Every row is the same string viewed from a different starting point.
decisions.lastColumnOutput: The last column is the Burrows-Wheeler output; the first column helps explain the sorted matrix structure.
decisions.lexicographicClusters: Lexicographic order is what makes the last column cluster similar characters together.
decisions.transformPreparesCompression: The transform did not compress by itself, but it concentrated repeated symbols so later stages can do it better.
decisions.upperMatrixSorted: The upper part of the matrix is already in final lexicographic order.

descriptions.collectLastColumn: Read the last column L = "{{lastColumn}}". Similar characters are now grouped much tighter than in the input.
descriptions.compareRows: Compare "{{current}}" with "{{previous}}" to place the next rotation lexicographically.
descriptions.complete: BWT finished. Output "{{lastColumn}}" creates {{outputRuns}} runs vs {{inputRuns}} in the original string.
descriptions.rowPlaced: Row "{{row}}" is now placed at sorted position {{position}}.

notes.characterFrequency: Higher frequency means a shorter prefix code and fewer bits per occurrence.
notes.initialHeap: Each leaf will become the bottom layer of the Huffman tree.
notes.newInternalNode: Left child gets '0', right child gets '1' in the code prefix.
notes.popTwoMinimums: The merged node frequency is the sum of its two children.
notes.prefixCodeSymbol: freq={{freq}} × {{bits}} bits = {{total}} bits for this symbol.

decisions.frequentShorterCodes: Characters that appear more frequently will get shorter codes.
decisions.mergeSmallestFirst: Always merge the two nodes with the smallest frequencies first.
decisions.minHeapReady: The min-heap always gives us the two lowest-frequency nodes to merge first.
decisions.pushMergedNode: Push the merged node back into the heap for the next round.
decisions.rootReady: Only one node left — this is the Huffman tree root!
decisions.tracePath: Trace the path from root to leaf '{{char}}': left = '0', right = '1'.

descriptions.buildHeap: Build initial min-heap from {{count}} leaf nodes, one per unique character.
descriptions.createdInternalNode: Created internal node with freq={{freq}}. Heap now has {{heapSize}} node(s).

notes.currentCompareMismatch: This is where KMP saves time with fail[j - 1].
notes.failureFallback: KMP reuses the best smaller border instead of rechecking from the start.
notes.failureJump: This is the KMP aha moment: reuse the longest safe prefix immediately.
notes.failureSeed: The first character has no shorter proper prefix to fall back to.
notes.failureValueBorder: This border becomes the next jump target.
notes.finalOutcome: KMP runs in O(n + m) because every fallback reuses the failure table instead of rescanning text.
notes.pointerUpdate: KMP only jumps on mismatches or after reporting a full hit.
notes.postMatchJump: Overlapping matches stay visible because the text cursor never moves backward.
notes.prefixCompare: Failure links store the longest reusable prefix when a mismatch happens later.
notes.readyToScan: Pattern starts under the first text character.
notes.textShift: Only the text moves when no failure link can help.

decisions.allMatchesFound: All matches were found without rewinding the text pointer.
decisions.buildFailureTable: Build the failure table before scanning the text.
decisions.eitherJumpOrMove: Either jump by failure or move the text cursor.
decisions.extendOverlap: Try to extend the current proper prefix / suffix overlap.
decisions.jumpByFailureLinks: The pattern can now jump by failure links instead of resetting after mismatches.
decisions.keepPaidWork: Slide inside the pattern and keep the work you already paid for.
decisions.noMatchFound: Failure jumps still prevented wasteful backtracking.
decisions.noReusablePrefix: No reusable prefix exists yet, so the window shifts by one.
decisions.preventedBacktracking: Failure jumps still prevented wasteful backtracking.
decisions.sameTextChar: Pattern slides under the same text character; the text pointer stays put.

descriptions.advance: Characters matched, so both pointers move forward together.
descriptions.compareFailureChars: Compare pattern[{{index}}] = "{{current}}" with prefix character pattern[{{prefix}}] = "{{prefixChar}}".
descriptions.completeMatches: KMP finished. Matches found at indices {{matches}}.
descriptions.completeNoMatch: KMP finished scanning the text. No full pattern match was found.
descriptions.failureBuildMismatch: Failure build mismatch: reuse fail[{{from}}] = {{next}} instead of restarting from zero.
descriptions.failureCommit: Set fail[{{index}}] = {{prefix}}. That overlap is ready for future jumps.
descriptions.failureJump: Mismatch. Jump pattern index from {{patternIndex}} to fail[{{failureIndex}}] = {{nextPatternIndex}} without moving the text cursor.
descriptions.failureTableReady: Failure table ready: [{{failure}}]. Start scanning the text without rewinding it.
descriptions.fullMatchFound: Full match found at text index {{hitStart}}. KMP keeps scanning by jumping j to fail[{{failureIndex}}] = {{fallback}}.
descriptions.prepare: Prepare KMP search for pattern "{{pattern}}" inside text "{{text}}".
descriptions.shiftText: Mismatch at j = 0, so only the text cursor moves to the next position.

notes.boundaryUpdate: Anything inside this boundary gets a free mirrored lower bound.
notes.expansionCompare: Each successful compare increases the palindrome radius by one transformed step.
notes.longestPalindrome: The longest original palindrome length is exactly the transformed radius.
notes.mirrorSeed: Manacher borrows the guaranteed safe part from the mirrored center before expanding.
notes.transformedString: This removes the need to separately handle odd and even palindrome centers.

decisions.addSeparators: Insert # separators so every palindrome behaves like an odd-length radius around one center.
decisions.allRadiiKnown: All palindrome radii are now known, so the final rainbow shows every center at once.
decisions.defineWindow: The current palindrome now defines the mirror window for future centers.
decisions.growOutward: Matching mirrored characters let the palindrome rainbow grow outward.
decisions.reuseMirror: Reuse the mirrored radius first; only extra growth needs fresh comparisons.

descriptions.complete: Manacher finished. The longest palindrome is "{{palindrome}}".
descriptions.mirrorReuse: Inside the current right boundary, copy min(right - i, P[mirror]) = {{reused}} from mirror {{mirror}}.
descriptions.shiftWindow: Update the active palindrome window to center {{center}} with right boundary {{right}}.
descriptions.transform: Transform "{{source}}" into "{{transformed}}" so odd and even palindromes share one unified expansion rule.

notes.collision: Rabin-Karp wins on average because these red-alert verifications are rare.
notes.finalOutcome: The rolling hash does the cheap filtering; character checks confirm the survivors.
notes.hashGateMatch: Hashes agree, so Rabin-Karp must verify the characters.
notes.hashGateSkip: No expensive per-character compare is needed here.
notes.initialHashes: If hashes differ, the whole window can be rejected instantly.
notes.rollingHash: Remove the old leading character, shift the base, and inject the new trailing one.
notes.verifiedHit: A hash match plus successful verification is a real occurrence.
notes.verifyCollision: This is the cost of a collision: hashes matched, text did not.
notes.verifyMatch: The hash candidate still looks legitimate.

decisions.collision: Equal hash, different text: this is a false alarm collision.
decisions.constantTimeRoll: Hash update costs O(1), so the window can glide without rehashing every character.
decisions.keepSliding: Keep sliding; the hash alone is not enough.
decisions.noMatchFound: Every promising hash window was either rejected or collided.
decisions.rollingHashNarrowed: Rolling hash narrowed the search to a few short verifications.
decisions.seedHashes: Hash the pattern and the first text window before sliding.
decisions.slideOnDifferentHash: Different hashes let the window slide immediately.
decisions.survivedVerification: The hash candidate survived every character check.
decisions.verifyOnEqualHash: Equal hashes trigger a character verification.

descriptions.compareHashes: Compare window hash {{windowHash}} with pattern hash {{patternHash}} at start {{start}}.
descriptions.completeMatches: Rabin-Karp finished. Verified matches at indices {{matches}}.
descriptions.completeNoMatch: Rabin-Karp finished. No verified pattern occurrence was found.
descriptions.falseAlarm: False alarm at window {{start}}: the rolling hash collided, but the characters do not fully match.
descriptions.prepare: Prepare Rabin-Karp for pattern "{{pattern}}" with base {{base}} and mod {{mod}}.
descriptions.rollHash: Roll the hash forward: drop "{{outgoingChar}}" and add "{{incomingChar}}".

notes.compressionSaves: Output is shorter than the input — RLE provided compression.
notes.emitRun: One (count, char) pair replaces an entire run in the output stream.
notes.extendRun: Each matching character increments the run counter by one.
notes.input: We will compress runs of repeated characters into count-char pairs.
notes.newRun: Starting count for this run. Will extend while the next char matches.
notes.noCompressionGain: Output is not shorter — RLE works best with long repeated runs.

decisions.compressionSaves: Compression saves space — the encoded output is shorter than the original.
decisions.eachRunPair: Each run of identical characters becomes a single (char, count) pair.
decisions.emitRun: The run ended at position {{endIndex}}. Emit ({{count}}, '{{char}}') to the output.
decisions.newRun: '{{char}}' begins a new run. Count how many consecutive '{{char}}' chars follow.
decisions.noCompressionGain: No compression gain — the encoded output is as long as or longer than the original.

descriptions.complete: RLE complete. {{runs}} runs found. Output: "{{output}}" ({{outputLength}} chars vs {{inputLength}} original → {{percent}}% ratio).
descriptions.scan: Position {{index}}: found '{{char}}'. Starting a new run.
descriptions.start: Start RLE on "{{text}}" ({{length}} chars). We scan left to right, grouping consecutive identical characters.

notes.boxReuse: The current [L, R] box already proves this much prefix match for free.
notes.boxUpdate: The current match becomes the new best reusable prefix window.
notes.combinedString: Any bar reaching the pattern length on the text side marks a full occurrence.
notes.expansionCompare: This extends the current prefix match by one more character.
notes.finalSkyline: Linear time comes from reusing the current [L, R] box instead of re-expanding every suffix from scratch.
notes.fullBar: This is why the Z skyline is so readable: full hits become obvious tall bars.

decisions.copyGuaranteedPart: Copy as much as the current box safely guarantees before expanding.
decisions.fullHeightBar: A full-height bar on the text side means the prefix pattern matched completely.
decisions.growTaller: As long as both sides match, the skyline bar grows taller.
decisions.noFullHeightBar: The skyline shows partial prefix reuse but never reaches the full pattern height.
decisions.reuseFutureWork: Future positions inside this box can reuse work instead of starting from zero.
decisions.revealAllMatches: The skyline now reveals every full occurrence at a glance.
decisions.skylineStartsFlat: The skyline starts flat; each bar will store a prefix length.

descriptions.buildArray: Build the Z-array for "{{combined}}" to reuse prefix matches across the whole string.
descriptions.completeMatches: Z-array complete. Full-pattern bars mark matches at indices {{matches}}.
descriptions.completeNoMatch: Z-array complete. No full pattern-height bar appeared on the text side.
descriptions.expandBar: Expand Z[{{index}}] by comparing prefix char "{{prefixChar}}" with "{{matchChar}}".
descriptions.patternHit: Bar Z[{{index}}] reached the full pattern length, so the pattern occurs in the text at index {{textOffset}}.
descriptions.reuseBox: Inside the active Z-box, seed Z[{{index}}] with min({{remaining}}, Z[{{sourceIndex}}]) = {{reused}}.
descriptions.shiftBox: Update the Z-box to [{{left}}, {{right}}] because the new bar extends farther right.

crews.description: Crew-to-task costs create several close assignment alternatives.
crews.label: Crews

labs.description: A denser cost matrix highlights why each mask encodes used jobs.
labs.label: Labs

robots.description: Assigning robots to charging bays shows subset masks filling from sparse to dense.
robots.label: Robots

classic.description: A compact coin-yield set shows why the last balloon in an interval matters.
classic.label: Classic

dense.description: A slightly larger board emphasizes interval reuse and split storage.
dense.label: Dense

zigzag.description: High-low alternation makes the split decisions visually dramatic.
zigzag.label: Zigzag

metro.description: A slightly noisier distance sequence shows why older lines become obsolete.
metro.label: Metro

pulse.description: Compact points keep the line transitions readable in a strip view.
pulse.label: Pulse

track.description: Monotone x-values make query order and hull pruning visually clean.
track.label: Track

metro.description: Urban stop labels frame the recurrence as a compact commuter problem.
metro.label: Metro

summit.description: A slightly taller climb makes the Fibonacci-like growth feel more dramatic.
summit.label: Summit

tower.description: A clean staircase count where each landing inherits the two previous counts.
tower.label: Tower

arcade.description: Medium denominations create several competing minimum-coin paths.
arcade.label: Arcade Tokens

market.description: Small and large coins mix to show reuse of the same row in unbounded DP.
market.label: Market Change

metro.description: Classic coin set where one greedy-looking pick is not always optimal.
metro.label: Metro Fare

freight.description: Heavier tails force the optimal split range to move steadily right.
freight.label: Freight

pods.description: Balanced numbers make monotone optimal splits easy to spot.
pods.label: Pods

shifts.description: Segmenting workloads into teams gives a clear partition-cost narrative.
shifts.label: Shifts

code.description: Programmer-ish tokens reveal how edits cluster around naming changes.
code.label: Code Refactor

plural.description: Word-shape changes make suffix edits and carry moves easy to follow.
plural.label: Grammar

typo.description: Classic misspellings show replace, insert and delete transitions cleanly.
typo.label: Typo Fix

classic.description: The standard Fibonacci sequence is ideal for showing additive DP growth.
classic.label: Classic

growth.description: A slightly larger n turns the recurrence into a visibly accelerating wave.
growth.label: Growth

signal.description: Shorter terms keep the focus on how the cache fills from left to right.
signal.label: Signal

camp.description: Balanced weights and values, ideal for showing skip vs take decisions.
camp.label: Camp Pack

heist.description: Several flashy high-value items compete for a tight capacity budget.
heist.label: Heist Loadout

lab.description: Dense scientific cargo where medium items often beat one expensive choice.
lab.label: Lab Crate

archives.description: Uneven file sizes make the saved split window more interesting.
archives.label: Archives

chapters.description: Adjacent file-merge costs produce a compact interval DP with narrow opt windows.
chapters.label: Chapters

packets.description: A short merge chain keeps the Knuth optimization readable and dense.
packets.label: Packets

dna.description: Short nucleotide strings make diagonal matches visually obvious.
dna.label: DNA

signals.description: Letter-code sequences create competing left vs top DP decisions.
signals.label: Signals

words.description: Readable word pairs make the final subsequence easy to understand.
words.label: Words

rebound.description: Late recovery values create a more dramatic backtrack route.
rebound.label: Rebound

skyline.description: Mostly rising values with a few breaks make the final subsequence easy to spot.
skyline.label: Skyline

wave.description: Alternating rises and dips force the algorithm to keep revising its best predecessor.
wave.label: Wave

phrase.description: Readable words make matching outer characters easier to follow.
phrase.label: Phrase

radar.description: Letter symmetry creates a visibly clean palindromic backbone.
radar.label: Radar

signal.description: The best palindrome is hidden across several skipped positions.
signal.label: Signal

balanced.description: Dimensions stay moderate and produce a steady triangular DP fill.
balanced.label: Balanced

classic.description: The textbook chain exposes why split points matter so much.
classic.label: Classic

swing.description: Alternating small and huge dimensions make bad split costs explode.
swing.label: Swing

corridor.description: A slightly longer board makes recurring profiles and counts easy to compare.
corridor.label: Corridor

lab.description: Compact profile states expose how a frontier DP reuses the same mask vocabulary.
lab.label: Lab Grid

tiles.description: Classic domino tilings show how frontier masks move from one column to the next.
tiles.label: Tiles

alias.description: Dot and star mix to show both literal matches and repeated spans.
alias.label: Alias

logs.description: A denser pattern makes zero-occurrence vs consume-one branches easy to compare.
logs.label: Logs

pulse.description: Short strings keep the regex star mechanic readable and compact.
pulse.label: Pulse

cache.description: A denser base array makes the sum-over-subsets propagation feel more dramatic.
cache.label: Cache

radar.description: Bitmask contributions are clustered, so each SOS stage lights up different families.
radar.label: Radar

signals.description: Subset values make it easy to see how supersets absorb all of their submasks.
signals.label: Signals

lab.description: A denser set makes the boolean table light up in clusters.
lab.label: Lab Budget

laser.description: Numbers are balanced so multiple near-target branches compete.
laser.label: Laser Grid

trail.description: Snack weights create one clean subset and a few tempting dead ends.
trail.label: Trail Mix

coast.description: A slightly larger route shows why subset masks need to remember the end city.
coast.label: Coast Hop

drone.description: Edge weights vary more sharply, so bad subset transitions stand out.
drone.label: Drone Route

metro.description: A compact symmetric city map that produces a clear optimal Hamiltonian tour.
metro.label: Metro Loop

campus.description: A wider tree creates clear postorder aggregation across several children.
campus.label: Campus

orchard.description: Fruit values on a branching tree make include-vs-skip choices easy to follow.
orchard.label: Orchard

vault.description: Heavier leaf rewards make the independent-set backtrack more dramatic.
vault.label: Vault

codes.description: Dense symbol-like strings make skipped vs consumed star states stand out.
codes.label: Codes

globs.description: Simple ? and * patterns show horizontal vs vertical star movement clearly.
globs.label: Globs

routes.description: Path-like strings make wildcard spans easy to reason about.
routes.label: Routes

banana.description: The canonical BWT demo: short, memorable, and perfect for grouped output runs.
banana.label: Banana

mississippi.description: Heavier repetition shows why BWT helps later run-length compression.
mississippi.label: Mississippi

panama.description: Readable phrase-derived string where rotations visibly cluster repeated letters.
panama.label: Panama

dna.description: Short genome-style pattern with repeated prefixes and suffixes.
dna.label: DNA

overlap.description: Classic self-overlap case where failure jumps save many retries.
overlap.label: Overlap

signal.description: Readable repeated phrase that makes jumps visually obvious.
signal.label: Signal

banana.description: Odd-length palindromes layer into a clean mirrored rainbow.
banana.label: Banana Glow

mixed.description: Odd and even palindromes compete, so the longest answer is not obvious up front.
mixed.label: Mixed

symmetry.description: Dense mirrored structure makes center reuse and boundary growth easy to see.
symmetry.label: Symmetry

alarm.description: Small modulus makes rolling hashes collide often enough to teach verification.
alarm.label: False Alarm

forensics.description: A repetitive string where rolling updates are easier to track than full rescans.
forensics.label: Forensics

neon.description: Readable repeated label stream with a clear matching window.
neon.label: Neon Feed

mixed.description: Mixture of short and long runs
mixed.label: Mixed

worst.description: String where RLE has no compression benefit
worst.label: No gain

classic.description: Pattern concatenation produces clear Z-box reuse and a couple of exact hits.
classic.label: Classic

genome.description: Many overlapping prefixes turn the Z skyline into a dense cityscape.
genome.label: Genome

log.description: Readable text with long repeated prefixes and visible full-pattern bars.
log.label: Log Stream

howItWorks.step1: Put the start into a priority queue with f = h(start).
howItWorks.step2: Pop the node with the smallest f; if it is the goal, reconstruct the path.
howItWorks.step3: Otherwise, relax each neighbour with g = current.g + edge cost and f = g + h(neighbour).
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until the goal is popped or the queue empties.

aStarPathfinding.keyIdea: Prioritise nodes by f = g (cost so far) + h (heuristic estimate of distance to goal) and expand the best first.

strengths.item1: Dramatically faster than Dijkstra when the heuristic points toward the goal.
strengths.item2: Still optimal if the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates the true distance).

aStarPathfinding.watch: The frontier hugs the goal direction because nodes with a bad h-score sit quietly in the queue.

weaknesses.item1: A bad heuristic degrades it to Dijkstra (or worse).
weaknesses.item2: Memory-hungry because every reached node stays in the open set.

howItWorks.step1: Set every distance to infinity except the source (0).
howItWorks.step2: Run V − 1 passes — in each pass relax every edge once.
howItWorks.step3: Do one extra pass: any distance that still decreases proves a reachable negative cycle.
howItWorks.step4: If no decrease happens, distances are correct shortest paths.

bellmanFord.keyIdea: Relax every edge up to V − 1 times; if one more pass still changes a distance, you have a negative cycle.

strengths.item1: Handles negative edge weights and detects negative cycles.
strengths.item2: Conceptually simple — no priority queue required.

bellmanFord.watch: The whole edge list is swept each pass — distances shorten everywhere, not just around one focus node.

weaknesses.item1: O(V · E) time — slower than Dijkstra on non-negative graphs.
weaknesses.item2: Not naturally incremental — adding an edge means replaying passes.

howItWorks.step1: Mark the source visited and push it into a FIFO queue.
howItWorks.step2: Pop the head; look at every neighbour that is still unseen.
howItWorks.step3: Mark each fresh neighbour visited, record the hop count, and push it to the back of the queue.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until the queue empties.

bfs.keyIdea: Explore the graph in waves: discover all neighbours of the source, then all of their neighbours, and so on.

strengths.item1: Finds the shortest path (in hops) on unweighted graphs in O(V + E).
strengths.item2: Easy to reason about — the queue literally tracks the next wave of work.

bfs.watch: The frontier expands in concentric rings — each wave is one more hop from the source.

weaknesses.item1: Ignores edge weights, so it is not a shortest-path algorithm on weighted graphs.
weaknesses.item2: Can exhaust memory on huge graphs because every frontier node sits in the queue at once.

howItWorks.step1: Pick an unvisited node, colour it A, push on a queue.
howItWorks.step2: Pop a node; colour every uncoloured neighbour the opposite colour and enqueue it.
howItWorks.step3: If a neighbour is already the same colour as the current node, the graph is NOT bipartite.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat from any remaining unvisited node for disconnected graphs.

bipartiteCheck.keyIdea: Try to colour every node with one of two colours so that no edge connects same-coloured nodes.

strengths.item1: O(V + E) time — single BFS per component.
strengths.item2: Doubles as a useful building block for matching problems.

bipartiteCheck.watch: Colours spread via BFS from each seed; a same-colour edge appears the moment the graph cannot be bipartite.

weaknesses.item1: Strictly binary — cannot generalise to k-colouring without changing approach.
weaknesses.item2: A conflict edge proves non-bipartite but does not locate all odd cycles.

howItWorks.step1: DFS the graph, recording discovery time and low-link (earliest reachable via back-edges) per node.
howItWorks.step2: Edge (u, v) is a bridge if low[v] > disc[u] — no back-edge gives an alternative route.
howItWorks.step3: Node u is an articulation point if it is the DFS root with ≥ 2 children, or if some child has low ≥ disc[u].
howItWorks.step4: Report every bridge and articulation point found.

bridgesArticulationPoints.keyIdea: DFS tracks discovery times and low-link values; an edge is a bridge and a node is an articulation point when removal disconnects the graph.

strengths.item1: Single O(V + E) DFS finds both bridges and articulation points.
strengths.item2: Directly identifies structural vulnerabilities in a network.

bridgesArticulationPoints.watch: Red nodes and edges flag the critical pieces — remove them and the graph falls apart.

weaknesses.item1: Low-link bookkeeping is delicate and easy to get wrong.
weaknesses.item2: Does not generalise to directed graphs without changes.

howItWorks.step1: Try k = 1, then 2, 3, … in turn.
howItWorks.step2: For each k, attempt to colour nodes one by one, assigning the smallest legal colour.
howItWorks.step3: On a conflict, back-track and try a different assignment.
howItWorks.step4: The first k that succeeds is the chromatic number.

chromaticNumber.keyIdea: Search for the smallest k such that the graph can be coloured with k colours so no edge connects same-coloured nodes.

strengths.item1: Conceptually straightforward — backtracking on a constraint-satisfaction problem.
strengths.item2: Works on arbitrary graphs, not just special families.

chromaticNumber.watch: Colours assign one-by-one; a red edge flags a conflict forcing the search to back-track and try another palette.

weaknesses.item1: Chromatic number is NP-hard — exponential in the worst case.
weaknesses.item2: Good pruning (e.g., colour by highest degree first) is essential for tolerable runtime.

howItWorks.step1: Walk the node list. If the next node is unvisited, start a traversal from it.
howItWorks.step2: DFS or BFS marks every node it reaches as belonging to this component.
howItWorks.step3: When the traversal ends, increment the component counter and move to the next unvisited node.
howItWorks.step4: Once every node is visited, you have grouped them all into components.

connectedComponents.keyIdea: Repeat DFS/BFS starting from each unvisited node; everything reached forms one connected component.

strengths.item1: O(V + E), straightforward implementation.
strengths.item2: Union-Find offers a near-constant online alternative if edges stream in over time.

connectedComponents.watch: Each unseen seed spawns a new coloured region — the number of colours is the component count.

weaknesses.item1: Does not preserve order — components are discovered in arbitrary order.
weaknesses.item2: Needs the full graph in memory.

howItWorks.step1: Track three colours per node: white (unseen), grey (on the DFS stack), black (fully processed).
howItWorks.step2: DFS: on entry mark grey, on exit mark black.
howItWorks.step3: If DFS sees an edge to a grey node — that is a back edge and proves a directed cycle.
howItWorks.step4: For undirected graphs, any non-parent edge to a seen node is a cycle.

cycleDetection.keyIdea: Run DFS and track whether each node is on the current path; finding a back-edge to a path node proves a cycle.

strengths.item1: O(V + E) and very simple to implement.
strengths.item2: Reports an actual cycle, not just a yes/no answer.

cycleDetection.watch: Nodes light up as "on stack" while the search descends; a back-edge completes the witness loop.

weaknesses.item1: DFS recursion depth can be a problem on deep graphs.
weaknesses.item2: Needs different logic for directed versus undirected graphs.

howItWorks.step1: Start at the source, mark it visited and push it on a stack (or recurse).
howItWorks.step2: Pick an unvisited neighbour and dive into it — repeat recursively.
howItWorks.step3: When a node has no unvisited neighbours, backtrack to its parent.
howItWorks.step4: Continue until the whole reachable component has been explored.

dfs.keyIdea: Go as deep as possible down one branch before backtracking and trying the next.

strengths.item1: O(V + E) and uses just O(h) stack space where h is the depth of the exploration.
strengths.item2: Natural fit for cycle detection, topological order, SCCs and bridge-finding.

dfs.watch: A long chain unfurls first; only when it dead-ends does the search jump back and branch sideways.

weaknesses.item1: Does NOT find shortest paths — order depends on which neighbour you pick first.
weaknesses.item2: Deep graphs can blow the call stack if implemented recursively without care.

howItWorks.step1: Set every distance to infinity except the source (0).
howItWorks.step2: Pull the unsettled node with the smallest distance from a min-heap.
howItWorks.step3: For each outgoing edge, relax: if going through the current node is cheaper, update the neighbour.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until every reachable node has been settled.

dijkstra.keyIdea: Greedily settle the unreached node with the smallest tentative distance, then relax its outgoing edges.

strengths.item1: Optimal shortest-path tree in O((V + E) log V) with a binary heap.
strengths.item2: Stops early if you only need the distance to one target.

dijkstra.watch: The settled node is always the current best pick — once settled, its distance is final.

weaknesses.item1: Assumes non-negative edge weights — a single negative edge can break correctness.
weaknesses.item2: Dense graphs benefit more from simpler O(V²) implementations than from a heap.

howItWorks.step1: BFS the residual graph from source to sink, assigning each node a level.
howItWorks.step2: If the sink is unreachable, you're done.
howItWorks.step3: DFS forward through edges that go strictly level by level, pushing blocking flow.
howItWorks.step4: Recompute the level graph and repeat until the sink is unreachable.

dinicMaxFlow.keyIdea: Build a level graph with BFS, then push "blocking flow" through all shortest augmenting paths at once via DFS.

strengths.item1: O(V²E) in general, O(E · √V) on bipartite / unit-capacity graphs — faster than Edmonds-Karp on most inputs.
strengths.item2: Blocking-flow DFS saturates many paths per phase.

dinicMaxFlow.watch: Nodes group into BFS levels; DFS saturates every s→t shortest path in one phase before the level graph rebuilds.

weaknesses.item1: More complex to implement than plain Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp.
weaknesses.item2: Cost-oblivious — extend to min-cost flow for weighted problems.

howItWorks.step1: Compute the reverse post-order of the CFG from the entry node.
howItWorks.step2: Initialise dom[entry] = entry; everything else unknown.
howItWorks.step3: Iterate: dom[B] = intersection of dom sets of all B's predecessors, plus B itself.
howItWorks.step4: When dom stops changing, the immediate dominator of B is the nearest strictly-dominating node.

dominatorTree.keyIdea: In a control-flow graph, node D dominates B if every path from entry to B goes through D — build a tree of immediate dominators.

strengths.item1: Near-linear in practice with the Cooper-Harvey-Kennedy algorithm.
strengths.item2: Core to compiler optimisations — loops, SSA construction, code-motion.

dominatorTree.watch: Dominator edges appear once every block's dominator set has stabilised.

weaknesses.item1: Only meaningful on reducible control-flow graphs or with Lengauer-Tarjan for speed.
weaknesses.item2: Subtle edge-cases around unreachable code and exit nodes.

howItWorks.step1: Build the residual graph (original capacities minus current flow).
howItWorks.step2: BFS from source to sink in the residual graph.
howItWorks.step3: Find the bottleneck capacity along the path and push that much flow.
howItWorks.step4: Update residual capacities. Repeat until no augmenting path exists.

edmondsKarp.keyIdea: Repeatedly find a shortest augmenting path via BFS in the residual graph and push flow along it until none remains.

strengths.item1: O(V · E²) polynomial time — predictable upper bound.
strengths.item2: BFS gives fewer phases than Ford-Fulkerson's arbitrary path picks.

edmondsKarp.watch: Each BFS lights up a shortest path from source to sink; once drawn, it gets saturated and disappears.

weaknesses.item1: Slower than Dinic on dense networks.
weaknesses.item2: Does not handle costs — fine for plain max flow only.

howItWorks.step1: Check degree conditions: Euler circuit needs all-even degrees, Euler path needs exactly two odd endpoints.
howItWorks.step2: Start at any even-degree node (or an odd endpoint for a path) and follow edges, consuming each once.
howItWorks.step3: If you hit a dead end, back-stitch a new sub-cycle from any revisited node with remaining unused edges.
howItWorks.step4: Splice sub-cycles into the main trail until every edge is consumed.

eulerPathCircuit.keyIdea: Find a walk that uses every edge exactly once, stitching the trail out of smaller sub-cycles (Hierholzer).

strengths.item1: O(E) linear time with Hierholzer's algorithm.
strengths.item2: Works on directed graphs with the analogous in-degree / out-degree conditions.

eulerPathCircuit.watch: Purple edges extend the live walk; teal edges are locked into the final trail as it grows.

weaknesses.item1: Only exists on graphs meeting the degree conditions — otherwise no trail exists.
weaknesses.item2: Must track unused edges carefully — naive visited-edge tracking can mis-route.

howItWorks.step1: Pick a seed cell and record its original colour.
howItWorks.step2: Add the seed to a queue (BFS) or stack (DFS).
howItWorks.step3: Pop a cell, repaint it, and enqueue every 4- or 8-neighbour that still has the original colour.
howItWorks.step4: Stop when the queue / stack is empty — the region is fully painted.

floodFill.keyIdea: Starting from a seed cell, paint every connected cell that matches the seed colour until the region is filled.

strengths.item1: Linear in the region size.
strengths.item2: Trivial to adapt to different adjacency (4-way, 8-way, diagonals).

floodFill.watch: The fill expands in waves from the seed — like a paint bucket in an image editor.

weaknesses.item1: Large regions on deep grids blow the recursion stack — prefer an explicit queue / stack.
weaknesses.item2: Does not preserve anti-aliased boundaries — works on sharp-edged regions.

howItWorks.step1: Start with dist[i][j] = edge weight (or infinity if no edge).
howItWorks.step2: For each candidate intermediate k, update dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]).
howItWorks.step3: After all V passes, dist holds every shortest path length between every pair.
howItWorks.step4: To recover paths, keep a parent/next matrix updated alongside.

floydWarshall.keyIdea: Allow paths through one more intermediate node k per outer pass; take the minimum of "direct i→j" and "i→k→j".

strengths.item1: Answers every shortest-path query in O(1) after O(V³) preprocessing — great when the graph is dense or queries are frequent.
strengths.item2: Handles negative weights (though still not negative cycles).

floydWarshall.watch: With each k pass, the pivot row and column fix their values and improvements ripple through the rest of the table.

weaknesses.item1: O(V³) time and O(V²) memory — impractical for very large V.
weaknesses.item2: Not suited if you need one source only — Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford is faster.

howItWorks.step1: BFS from every free left vertex, building alternating layers of matched / unmatched edges.
howItWorks.step2: Stop the BFS at the first layer containing a free right vertex.
howItWorks.step3: DFS back through the layers, augmenting along vertex-disjoint shortest paths.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until BFS can no longer reach a free right vertex.

hopcroftKarp.keyIdea: For bipartite graphs, find many shortest vertex-disjoint augmenting paths per phase using a BFS + DFS pair.

strengths.item1: O(E · √V) — the fastest known algorithm for bipartite matching.
strengths.item2: Natural fit for assignment, bipartite covers and job-scheduling problems.

hopcroftKarp.watch: BFS builds alternating layers between free left and right vertices; DFS threads augmenting paths through them.

weaknesses.item1: Only works on bipartite graphs.
weaknesses.item2: Layered bookkeeping adds implementation complexity over simpler flow-based matching.

howItWorks.step1: Subtract each row's minimum from that row; then each column's minimum from that column.
howItWorks.step2: Cover all zeros with the minimum number of horizontal / vertical lines.
howItWorks.step3: If lines = n, assign zeros to form a perfect matching — done.
howItWorks.step4: Otherwise find the smallest uncovered value, subtract it from uncovered rows, add it to doubly-covered columns, and retry.

hungarianAlgorithm.keyIdea: Solve minimum-cost bipartite assignment by reducing the cost matrix, covering all zeros with the fewest lines, and adjusting until a perfect matching fits.

strengths.item1: O(n³) polynomial — dramatically better than brute-force O(n!).
strengths.item2: Produces an optimal assignment, not an approximation.

hungarianAlgorithm.watch: Cover lines cross the matrix; when the count equals n you can pick a valid assignment of zeros.

weaknesses.item1: The line-cover step is subtle to implement correctly.
weaknesses.item2: Works on square cost matrices — pad with dummy rows/cols for rectangular inputs.

howItWorks.step1: DFS the original graph and push each node on a stack as it finishes.
howItWorks.step2: Reverse every edge.
howItWorks.step3: Pop nodes off the stack; whenever you pop an unvisited node, DFS it in the reversed graph — the reached set is one SCC.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until the stack is empty.

kosarajuScc.keyIdea: First DFS records finish order; second DFS on the reversed graph, in reverse finish order, peels off one SCC per traversal.

strengths.item1: Conceptually clean: two ordinary DFS runs, no low-link tracking.
strengths.item2: Easy to explain and debug.

kosarajuScc.watch: Pass 1 builds a stack of finish times. Pass 2 walks reversed edges — each DFS call discovers exactly one SCC.

weaknesses.item1: Needs the reversed graph — extra time + memory.
weaknesses.item2: Two passes versus Tarjan's one.

howItWorks.step1: Sort every edge by weight (ascending).
howItWorks.step2: Walk the list; for each edge, check if its endpoints are in different components.
howItWorks.step3: If yes — accept it (add to MST, union the components). If no — reject it.
howItWorks.step4: Stop once V − 1 edges have been accepted — that is the MST.

kruskalsMst.keyIdea: Sort all edges by weight; pick the cheapest one that does not close a cycle, using Union-Find to check.

strengths.item1: O(E log E) time, dominated by the sort; linear once edges are sorted.
strengths.item2: Works cleanly on disconnected graphs (you get a minimum spanning forest).

kruskalsMst.watch: Edges are offered in order — accepted ones join the MST, rejected ones would have closed a cycle.

weaknesses.item1: Needs a fast disjoint-set structure to hit optimal speed.
weaknesses.item2: Sorting the full edge list is wasted work if only a small prefix matters.

howItWorks.step1: Build the residual graph with capacities AND edge costs (negative on reverse edges).
howItWorks.step2: Find the cheapest s→t path via SPFA / Bellman-Ford (to allow negative reverse costs).
howItWorks.step3: Push the bottleneck flow along it, charging the cost × bottleneck.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until no augmenting path exists; sum of costs × flows is the answer.

minCostMaxFlow.keyIdea: Find the max flow whose total edge cost is minimum — repeatedly push flow along the cheapest augmenting path.

strengths.item1: Finds both the max flow and the minimum total cost in one procedure.
strengths.item2: Fits naturally on assignment, transportation and supply-chain problems.

minCostMaxFlow.watch: Each augmentation lights up the currently-cheapest s→t route (SPFA or Bellman-Ford on residual costs).

weaknesses.item1: Slower than plain max-flow: shortest-path step per augmentation.
weaknesses.item2: Negative costs in reverse edges require SPFA or Johnson-style reweighting.

howItWorks.step1: Pick any start node; add it to the tree.
howItWorks.step2: Put every edge leaving the tree into a min-heap, keyed by weight.
howItWorks.step3: Pop the cheapest edge; if its far endpoint is not in the tree yet, add both the edge and that endpoint.
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until every node is in the tree — you now have the MST.

primsMst.keyIdea: Start from any node and repeatedly add the cheapest edge that leaves the tree and reaches a new node.

strengths.item1: O(E log V) with a heap — fast on dense graphs.
strengths.item2: Easy to parallelise along the frontier.

primsMst.watch: The growing tree always hangs off one connected blob — edges crossing the boundary are the candidates.

weaknesses.item1: Only works on connected graphs — otherwise you only span one component.
weaknesses.item2: Implementation is more involved than Kruskal if you do not already have a heap.

howItWorks.step1: Enumerate subsets of terminals (up to 2^t subsets for t terminals).
howItWorks.step2: DP: cheapest tree spanning a given subset, rooted at a given node.
howItWorks.step3: Combine smaller subsets via tree merges and edge extensions (Dreyfus-Wagner).
howItWorks.step4: Answer = cheapest tree spanning the full terminal set, over all possible roots.

steinerTree.keyIdea: Find the cheapest tree that connects a chosen subset of terminal nodes, allowing optional Steiner helpers along the way.

strengths.item1: Exact solution — no heuristic approximation.
strengths.item2: Polynomial in graph size for a fixed number of terminals (Dreyfus-Wagner is O(3^t · V + 2^t · E log V)).

steinerTree.watch: Blue terminals must all connect; green nodes are optional helpers; teal edges form the growing terminal tree.

weaknesses.item1: Exponential in the number of terminals — impractical beyond ~20 terminals.
weaknesses.item2: Implementation is heavy compared to MST or shortest-path.

howItWorks.step1: DFS the graph, assigning each node an index (discovery order) and a low-link (lowest index reachable).
howItWorks.step2: Push each newly discovered node on an SCC stack.
howItWorks.step3: When you finish a node whose low-link equals its own index, pop everything above it off the stack — that is one SCC.
howItWorks.step4: Continue until every node is processed.

tarjanScc.keyIdea: Run a single DFS and track each node's low-link value; whenever low-link == index, an SCC closes at the current root.

strengths.item1: Single DFS — O(V + E).
strengths.item2: Does not need the reversed graph.

tarjanScc.watch: Nodes sit on an auxiliary stack during DFS; a whole SCC gets popped off in one burst when its root finishes.

weaknesses.item1: Low-link bookkeeping is easy to get wrong; off-by-one bugs are common.
weaknesses.item2: Conceptually denser than Kosaraju — harder to teach.

howItWorks.step1: Compute the in-degree of every node.
howItWorks.step2: Put every in-degree-0 node into a queue.
howItWorks.step3: Pop a node, append it to the result, and decrement in-degree on each outgoing neighbour.
howItWorks.step4: Push any neighbour whose in-degree just hit 0. Stop when the queue empties.

topologicalSortKahn.keyIdea: Repeatedly remove a node with zero in-degree; its edges vanish, freeing more zero-in-degree nodes to be taken next.

strengths.item1: O(V + E), iterative, no recursion.
strengths.item2: Detects cycles naturally — if the result is shorter than V, a cycle exists.

topologicalSortKahn.watch: A queue of "ready" nodes shrinks and refills as dependencies are satisfied.

weaknesses.item1: Only defined on DAGs — cyclic graphs have no topological order.
weaknesses.item2: The returned order is one valid order out of many; not deterministic unless you tie-break.

howItWorks.step1: Pre-order: node → left subtree → right subtree (top-down copy).
howItWorks.step2: In-order: left subtree → node → right subtree (sorted output on a BST).
howItWorks.step3: Post-order: left → right → node (safe for delete / cleanup).
howItWorks.step4: Level-order: queue-based wave by wave (the tree equivalent of BFS).

treeTraversals.keyIdea: Visit every node of a tree in a chosen order — pre-order, in-order, post-order, or level-order (BFS).

strengths.item1: Linear O(n) time and O(h) space for DFS-style traversals; O(w) space for level-order.
strengths.item2: Every traversal exposes a different natural structural property of the tree.

treeTraversals.watch: The visit order is what matters — watch which node prints its label first, before or after its children.

weaknesses.item1: Recursive DFS traversals can blow the stack on very deep trees — switch to iterative + explicit stack.
weaknesses.item2: Order matters — swapping left/right can silently break downstream consumers expecting a specific traversal.

howItWorks.step1: Each element starts as its own set — its parent pointer points to itself.
howItWorks.step2: Find(x): follow parent pointers until you hit a root. Compress the path so future finds are faster.
howItWorks.step3: Union(a, b): find both roots; hang the smaller tree under the larger root.
howItWorks.step4: After enough ops, nearly all nodes point directly at the root.

unionFind.keyIdea: Maintain a forest of disjoint sets with two ops: find the root of a set, and union two sets into one.

strengths.item1: With path compression and union by rank, operations are effectively O(α(n)) — near constant.
strengths.item2: Perfect fit for Kruskal, connectivity queries and offline connectivity checks.

unionFind.watch: Find walks up to the root; union re-parents one root under the other and path-compression flattens the chain.

weaknesses.item1: Does not support deletion or split — the tree only ever merges.
weaknesses.item2: Path-compression mutates structure during read-only-looking find calls.

howItWorks.step1: Walk left-to-right, comparing each pair of neighbours.
howItWorks.step2: If the left value is bigger, swap them.
howItWorks.step3: After a full pass the largest unsorted value settles at the right end.
howItWorks.step4: Shrink the pass range by one and repeat — stop when a whole pass has no swaps.

bubbleSort.keyIdea: Repeatedly swap adjacent pairs. After each pass the largest unsorted value "bubbles" to the right end.

strengths.item1: Easy to implement and very easy to explain.
strengths.item2: Runs in O(n) on input that is already sorted — the early-exit check catches it.

bubbleSort.watch: Watch the right edge: every pass locks in one more final element.

weaknesses.item1: O(n²) on average — impractical for anything beyond small inputs.
weaknesses.item2: Every swap touches adjacent memory only, so it has no structural advantages over insertion sort.

howItWorks.step1: Split the value range into a fixed number of buckets.
howItWorks.step2: Drop each value into the bucket that covers its range.
howItWorks.step3: Sort each bucket independently (usually with insertion sort).
howItWorks.step4: Concatenate the buckets in order to get the final sorted array.

bucketSort.keyIdea: Partition values into buckets by range, sort each bucket independently, then concatenate the buckets.

strengths.item1: Close to O(n) when values are uniformly distributed.
strengths.item2: Stable if the inner bucket sort is stable.

bucketSort.watch: Works best when values are evenly distributed so each bucket stays small.

weaknesses.item1: Degrades to O(n²) if many values cluster in a single bucket.
weaknesses.item2: Extra memory proportional to the number of buckets.

howItWorks.step1: Scan the array once and count how many times each value appears.
howItWorks.step2: Turn the counts into cumulative positions in the output.
howItWorks.step3: Walk the input again and place each value at its reserved output index.
howItWorks.step4: Copy the output back into the array.

countingSort.keyIdea: Count how many times each value appears, then use those counts to place each value directly in the output.

strengths.item1: Linear time: O(n + k), where k = size of the value range.
strengths.item2: Stable and comparison-free.

countingSort.watch: No comparisons happen — the algorithm trades memory for speed.

weaknesses.item1: Needs O(k) extra memory — impractical when the value range is very large.
weaknesses.item2: Only works for integer-like, bounded keys.

howItWorks.step1: Rearrange the array into a max-heap — every parent ≥ its children.
howItWorks.step2: Swap the root (the max) with the last heap element.
howItWorks.step3: Shrink the heap by one and restore the heap property on the new root (sift-down).
howItWorks.step4: Repeat until the heap is empty.

heapSort.keyIdea: Build a max-heap, then repeatedly swap the root with the last element and shrink the heap.

strengths.item1: Guaranteed O(n log n), in-place, constant extra memory.
strengths.item2: Worst-case behaviour matches the average — no adversarial inputs.

heapSort.watch: The heap region contracts from the right while the sorted suffix grows.

weaknesses.item1: Cache-unfriendly index jumps — slower in practice than quick/merge sort.
weaknesses.item2: Not stable.

howItWorks.step1: Treat the first element as a sorted prefix of length 1.
howItWorks.step2: Pick the next element from the unsorted region.
howItWorks.step3: Shift larger sorted values one step right until the pick fits.
howItWorks.step4: Drop the pick into the gap. Repeat until nothing is left to insert.

insertionSort.keyIdea: Take the next element and slide it leftward into its place inside the already-sorted prefix.

strengths.item1: Near-linear (O(n)) on nearly-sorted input — excellent for "mostly sorted" data.
strengths.item2: Stable, in-place, and simple enough to hand-execute.

insertionSort.watch: The sorted prefix keeps its order; neighbours shift right to make room.

weaknesses.item1: O(n²) on random or reverse-sorted input.
weaknesses.item2: Lots of small shifts — cache-friendly but slow for large arrays.

howItWorks.step1: Split the array into two halves.
howItWorks.step2: Recursively sort each half with the same procedure.
howItWorks.step3: Walk both sorted halves in parallel, copying the smaller head into the output.
howItWorks.step4: Continue until both halves are drained into the merged result.

mergeSort.keyIdea: Split the array in half, sort each half recursively, then merge the sorted halves into one sorted array.

strengths.item1: Guaranteed O(n log n) time on every input.
strengths.item2: Stable — equal values keep their original order.

mergeSort.watch: Small sub-arrays become sorted first; merges combine them upward.

weaknesses.item1: Needs O(n) extra memory for the merge buffer.
weaknesses.item2: Slower in practice than quick sort due to the extra copying.

howItWorks.step1: Pick a pivot element (first, last, middle, or random).
howItWorks.step2: Partition: move every value smaller than the pivot to its left, every larger value to its right.
howItWorks.step3: The pivot now sits at its final sorted position.
howItWorks.step4: Recurse into the left partition and the right partition.

quickSort.keyIdea: Pick a pivot, partition smaller values left and larger values right, then recurse into each side.

strengths.item1: Often the fastest in-practice comparison sort — great cache behaviour.
strengths.item2: In-place with O(log n) recursion depth when pivots split evenly.

quickSort.watch: After every partition, the pivot lands at its final position.

weaknesses.item1: O(n²) in the worst case when pivots split the array badly.
weaknesses.item2: Not stable by default; randomised pivots mitigate adversarial inputs.

howItWorks.step1: Find the number with the most digits — this sets the number of passes.
howItWorks.step2: Sort the array by the least-significant digit using a stable bucket sort.
howItWorks.step3: Repeat for each more-significant digit.
howItWorks.step4: After the most-significant digit pass, the whole array is sorted.

radixSort.keyIdea: Sort by one digit position at a time, from least significant to most significant.

strengths.item1: Beats O(n log n) for fixed-width keys — runs in O(nk), where k = digit count.
strengths.item2: Stable, so it is safe to use as the inner sort of a bigger pipeline.

radixSort.watch: Each digit pass preserves the order established by previous passes.

weaknesses.item1: Only works out-of-the-box for non-negative integers or bounded keys.
weaknesses.item2: Extra memory for the bucket arrays on every pass.

howItWorks.step1: Treat the whole array as one unsorted region.
howItWorks.step2: Scan the unsorted region for the smallest value.
howItWorks.step3: Swap that smallest value into the first unsorted slot.
howItWorks.step4: Move the sorted-boundary one step right and repeat.

selectionSort.keyIdea: Scan the unsorted region for the minimum, then swap it to the front of that region.

strengths.item1: Uses at most n − 1 swaps — the fewest of any O(n²) sort.
strengths.item2: Constant extra memory and very simple control flow.

selectionSort.watch: Watch the left edge grow by exactly one sorted element each pass.

weaknesses.item1: Always O(n²) — there is no early exit on already-sorted input.
weaknesses.item2: Not stable by default: equal values can change their relative order.

howItWorks.step1: Pick a gap sequence (for example n/2, n/4, …, 1).
howItWorks.step2: For each gap, run insertion sort on the subsequences that are exactly that far apart.
howItWorks.step3: Move to the next, smaller gap and repeat.
howItWorks.step4: Finish with a gap = 1 pass — a final insertion sort on the whole array.

shellSort.keyIdea: Run insertion sort on subsequences spaced by a gap; shrink the gap and repeat until gap = 1.

strengths.item1: Much faster than plain insertion sort on random input — usually O(n^1.25) to O(n^1.5).
strengths.item2: Simple, in-place, no recursion.

shellSort.watch: Larger gaps fix long-range disorder; the final gap = 1 pass polishes.

weaknesses.item1: Exact complexity depends on the gap sequence, and analysing it is surprisingly hard.
weaknesses.item2: Not stable.

howItWorks.step1: Scan the array for monotonic runs (ascending or descending sequences).
howItWorks.step2: Pad runs that are shorter than a minimum using insertion sort.
howItWorks.step3: Push each run on a stack and merge while a size-balance invariant is violated.
howItWorks.step4: When the scan ends, drain the stack by merging the remaining runs.

timSort.keyIdea: Detect already-sorted "runs", sort short ones with insertion, then merge runs with a stack policy.

strengths.item1: O(n) on sorted or nearly-sorted input — the runs are reused as-is.
strengths.item2: Stable; shipped as the default sort in Python, Java, Android, and many others.

timSort.watch: Naturally ordered regions are preserved and merged without re-sorting.

weaknesses.item1: The implementation is much more complex than merge or quick sort.
weaknesses.item2: Small O(n) extra memory for the merge buffer.

dp.emptyLabel: No DP trace available for this step.
dp.guideDesc: Lock onto the active row, column or interval, then use the highlighted chips to understand which subproblems feed the next write.

knapsack.packEmptyLabel: Selected items appear here during backtracking.

grid.decisionFallback: watch the board evolve step by step
grid.guideFallbackDesc: Watch the active frontier cell and the route it unlocks next.
grid.snapshotDesc: Source, target and wave stats stay separate from the board so the motion on the grid stays readable.

matrix.emptyLabel: No matrix trace available for this step.
matrix.guideDesc: Follow the highlighted rows, columns and pivot cells first. The rest of the matrix is supporting context.
matrix.snapshotDesc: Current phase, pivot and result stay visible without competing with the board itself.

network.emptyLabel: No layered network trace available for this step.
network.guideDesc: Status, decision and focus chips tell you whether the current pass is searching, augmenting or locking a path.
network.snapshotDesc: Route pressure, queue preview and result stay readable without covering the actual network.

search.decisionFallback: watch the signal sweep the array
search.guideFallbackDesc: Track the active probe before you scan the rest of the array.
search.snapshotDesc: Window bounds, probe position and confirmed hits stay in one compact overview.

string.computationEmptyNote: This step is a pure state transition with no extra formula to explain.
string.emptyLabel: No String Lab trace available for this step.

page.emptyLabel: No structures match the current filters.
page.heroDescription: A cleaner overview of foundational structures, grouped by learning track and ready for future interactive modules.
